name: Modulo Scheduling
summary: Rau (HP Labs 1994): transform an inner loop so that iterations overlap — iteration i+1 starts while iteration i is still finishing — exposing more ILP across iteration boundaries. Find the minimum initiation interval (II) and generate prologue/kernel/epilogue. Pays off on wide-issue architectures with high register count.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Software pipelining transforms an inner loop so that iterations overlap — iteration *i+1* starts executing while iteration *i* is still finishing — exposing more ILP across iteration boundaries. **Modulo scheduling** (Rau, HP Labs 1994) is the classical algorithm: find the minimum initiation interval (II) such that the loop body can be scheduled periodically with period II, then generate prologue/kernel/epilogue.

sections.title: Algorithm
sections.content:
Compute the minimum **initiation interval** (II) — the smallest cycle count between iteration starts that respects both data dependences and resource constraints. Data dependences in a loop carry from one iteration to the next via loop-carried values; resource constraints come from finite hardware (functional units, registers).

Given II, schedule one iteration of the loop body modulo II. The schedule is a function from each instruction to (cycle, resource), with the constraint that two instructions that conflict in their resource use cannot be at the same `cycle mod II`.

Generate three regions: a **prologue** that fills the pipeline by starting II-many iterations before the steady state, a **kernel** that is the modulo-scheduled body running periodically, and an **epilogue** that drains the pipeline after the last iteration starts. The transformation overlaps independent iterations, exploiting parallelism that single-iteration scheduling cannot.

sections.title: Production relevance
sections.content:
Modulo scheduling pays off most on architectures with high register count and wide issue (IA-64, modern GPUs, some DSPs). LLVM's `MachinePipeliner` implements it for targets that opt in.

On general-purpose CPUs with out-of-order execution, much of the benefit is captured by the hardware scheduler, so explicit software pipelining is often redundant — which is why it is an optional, target-specific pass rather than a universal one. On in-order or VLIW targets without aggressive hardware reordering, modulo scheduling remains essential and produces dramatic speedups on the inner loops it can handle.
